cases.title: Distal esophagus biopsy: Barrett esophagus, negative for dysplasia
cases.clinicalHistory: 59-year-old man with chronic reflux and a 2 cm segment of columnar-lined distal esophagus on surveillance endoscopy.

image.caption: Barrett esophagus with specialized intestinal metaplasia and goblet cells.

diagnosticSteps: Confirm the biopsy is from tubular esophagus or gastroesophageal junction mucosa with columnar epithelium.
Identify true goblet cells to support specialized intestinal metaplasia.
Assess crypt architecture, surface maturation, and nuclear atypia to determine whether dysplasia is present.

ancillaryOptions.name: No ancillary study
ancillaryOptions.result: Morphology is sufficient for uncomplicated Barrett esophagus without dysplasia.

ancillaryOptions.name: p53 immunostain
ancillaryOptions.result: Reserve for difficult dysplasia assessment; aberrant staining can support true dysplasia in the correct morphology.

ancillaryOptions.name: Alcian blue/PAS
ancillaryOptions.result: Can highlight acidic mucin but is not required when goblet cells are evident on H&E.

cases.reportingTarget: Esophagus, distal, biopsy: Barrett esophagus with intestinal metaplasia, negative for dysplasia.
pitfalls: Do not diagnose Barrett esophagus from gastric cardia intestinal metaplasia without endoscopic context.
Do not overcall reactive basal nuclear changes adjacent to erosion as low-grade dysplasia.

cases.title: Duodenal biopsy: celiac-type mucosal injury
cases.specimenType: Duodenal bulb and second portion biopsies
cases.clinicalHistory: 34-year-old woman with iron-deficiency anemia, chronic bloating, and elevated tissue transglutaminase IgA.

image.caption: Duodenal mucosa with villous blunting and increased intraepithelial lymphocytes, compatible with celiac-type injury.

diagnosticSteps: Orient villi and crypts before grading villous blunting to avoid tangential artifact.
Count intraepithelial lymphocytes and assess crypt hyperplasia, lamina propria inflammation, and epithelial injury.
Correlate with serology and gluten exposure because the histologic pattern is not specific for celiac disease.

ancillaryOptions.name: CD3 immunostain
ancillaryOptions.result: Highlights increased intraepithelial T lymphocytes when the H&E count is borderline.

ancillaryOptions.name: H. pylori stain on gastric biopsies
ancillaryOptions.result: Consider if peptic duodenitis is in the differential based on concurrent gastric sampling.

ancillaryOptions.name: Giardia evaluation
ancillaryOptions.result: Review luminal surface for organisms when symptoms or travel history suggest infection.

cases.reportingTarget: Duodenum, biopsy: Duodenal mucosa with villous blunting, crypt hyperplasia, and increased intraepithelial lymphocytes, compatible with celiac-type mucosal injury in the appropriate clinical setting.
pitfalls: Do not diagnose celiac disease as a stand-alone pathologic certainty without serologic and dietary context.
Do not grade villous architecture on poorly oriented fragments.

cases.title: Right colon polypectomy: sessile serrated lesion without dysplasia
cases.clinicalHistory: 62-year-old woman undergoing screening colonoscopy with a flat 9 mm mucus-capped polyp in the ascending colon.

image.caption: Sessile serrated lesion with dilated and horizontally branched basal crypts.

diagnosticSteps: Identify serrated architecture extending to the crypt base with basal dilatation, branching, or horizontal growth.
Separate sessile serrated lesion from hyperplastic polyp using location, size, and basal crypt morphology.
Search for conventional or serrated dysplasia and assess whether the cauterized margin is evaluable.

ancillaryOptions.name: No ancillary study
ancillaryOptions.result: Diagnosis is based on crypt architecture and dysplasia assessment.

ancillaryOptions.name: MLH1 immunostain
ancillaryOptions.result: Not needed without dysplasia; loss may support serrated-pathway dysplasia when present.

ancillaryOptions.name: BRAF V600E testing
ancillaryOptions.result: Not required for routine polypectomy sign-out.

cases.reportingTarget: Colon, ascending, polypectomy: Sessile serrated lesion, negative for dysplasia.
pitfalls: Do not call a right-sided SSL hyperplastic polyp when abnormal basal crypts are present.

cases.title: Right hemicolectomy: invasive colonic adenocarcinoma
cases.clinicalHistory: 70-year-old man with iron-deficiency anemia and an obstructing cecal mass biopsied as adenocarcinoma.

image.caption: Colorectal adenocarcinoma with infiltrative malignant glands in desmoplastic stroma.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: distribution of length of stay los of 97 patients undergoing minimally invasive right hemicolectomy with intracorporeal or extracorporeal anastomosis ia intracorporeal anastomosis ea extracorporeal anastomosis

diagnosticSteps: Confirm invasive adenocarcinoma and assign histologic grade, including mucinous, signet-ring, or medullary features if present.
Determine deepest anatomic extent, lymphovascular invasion, perineural invasion, tumor budding if applicable, and margin status.
Count examined and positive lymph nodes and integrate mismatch repair screening results for reporting.

ancillaryOptions.name: MLH1, PMS2, MSH2, MSH6 immunohistochemistry
ancillaryOptions.result: Universal MMR screening is recommended for colorectal adenocarcinoma.

ancillaryOptions.name: BRAF V600E or MLH1 promoter methylation
ancillaryOptions.result: Reflex testing is useful when MLH1/PMS2 loss is identified.

ancillaryOptions.name: HER2 testing
ancillaryOptions.result: Consider in advanced/metastatic RAS wild-type disease, not required for primary resection sign-out.

cases.reportingTarget: Colon, right hemicolectomy: Moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of cecum, 4.2 cm, invading through muscularis propria into pericolonic tissue, margins negative, 2 of 24 lymph nodes positive, pT3 pN1b. MMR immunohistochemistry pending.
pitfalls: Do not finalize staging without total lymph node count and positive node count.
Do not omit treatment effect if the patient received neoadjuvant therapy.

cases.title: Subtotal gastrectomy: poorly cohesive gastric adenocarcinoma
cases.clinicalHistory: 66-year-old woman with weight loss and a poorly distensible antrum; biopsy showed poorly cohesive carcinoma with signet-ring cells.

image.caption: Poorly cohesive gastric adenocarcinoma with signet-ring cell morphology.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: endoscopic radiologic and pathologic findings of case 2 a esophagogastroduodenoscopy showed a polypoid mass without ulceration in the gastric antrum b an initial abdominal computed tomography revealed a 2 0 cm sized protruding lesion arrow in the proximal antrum and no enlarged perigastric lymph node c gross examination of the distal subtotal gastrectomy specimen showed 2 3 cm 1 5 cm sized elevated lesion of early gastric cancer type i arrow in the anterior wall of the antrum d serial sectioning revealed that the tumor was limited to the mucosa e low power microscopic view of the tumor showed differentiated type adenocarcinoma with intact muscularis mucosae hematoxylin eosin he stain 12 5 f high power view revealed up to five foci of tumor buddings arrows at the invasive front he stain 200 g although no lymphatic invasion was seen in the primary tumor lymph node metastases arrows were found in perigastric lymph nodes he stain 12 5 h high power view of the lymph nodes showed metastatic gastric adenocarcinoma he stain 200

diagnosticSteps: Confirm poorly cohesive adenocarcinoma and document signet-ring cell predominance if present.
Map tumor depth through gastric wall layers and assess proximal, distal, and radial/omental margins.
Report lymphovascular invasion, perineural invasion, treatment effect if applicable, lymph nodes, and biomarker status.

ancillaryOptions.name: HER2 immunohistochemistry with reflex ISH if equivocal
ancillaryOptions.result: Recommended predictive biomarker for gastric/GEJ adenocarcinoma in appropriate clinical settings.

ancillaryOptions.name: Mismatch repair immunohistochemistry
ancillaryOptions.result: Assesses deficient MMR/MSI phenotype with therapeutic and hereditary implications.

ancillaryOptions.name: EBER in situ hybridization
ancillaryOptions.result: Can identify EBV-associated gastric carcinoma when morphology or institutional protocol supports testing.

cases.reportingTarget: Stomach, distal subtotal gastrectomy: Poorly cohesive adenocarcinoma with signet-ring cell features, 5.1 cm, invading subserosal connective tissue, margins negative, lymphovascular and perineural invasion present, 7 of 28 lymph nodes positive, pT3 pN3a.
pitfalls: Do not underestimate extent in diffuse poorly cohesive carcinoma with subtle gross borders.
Do not omit biomarker testing status when local protocol requires it.

cases.title: Gallbladder: cholelithiasis, gross-only workflow
cases.clinicalHistory: 45-year-old woman with biliary colic and ultrasound showing multiple mobile gallstones without mass or wall thickening.

image.caption: Gallbladder with cholelithiasis and no described mass lesion.

diagnosticSteps: Confirm the specimen is an uncomplicated gallbladder with expected anatomy, cystic duct margin, mucosa, wall, and stones.
Inspect for mass, polyp, mucosal ulceration, porcelain change, focal wall thickening, or clinical concern that would require histologic sampling.
Document stone burden and gross wall/mucosal findings according to local gross-only criteria.

ancillaryOptions.name: No ancillary study
ancillaryOptions.result: No ancillary study is required for an uncomplicated gross-only gallbladder.

ancillaryOptions.name: Histologic sections
ancillaryOptions.result: Recommended if a mass, suspicious mucosal lesion, marked wall thickening, or unexpected clinical concern is present.

cases.reportingTarget: Gallbladder, cholecystectomy: Cholelithiasis. No gross mass lesion identified.
pitfalls: Do not use a gross-only workflow if a mass, polyp, or focal wall thickening is present.
Do not discard the cystic duct margin description when local policy requires it.
